mappings.rationaleAI: The Heart covers the history of understanding blood circulation from Galen through Harvey's 1628 discovery, cardiac surgery, artificial hearts, and heart transplantation -- directly relevant to the heart's structure and blood circulation.

Blood covers blood components (red cells, white cells, platelets, plasma), blood typing and transfusion, and Harvey's discovery of circulation -- directly relevant to human blood components, blood groups, blood clotting, and circulation.

The nervous system covers central and peripheral nervous systems, sensory and motor pathways, reflex arcs, the autonomic nervous system (sympathetic and parasympathetic), and synaptic transmission -- directly mapping to the outline's nervous system sections.

Hormones covers major hormones including insulin, adrenaline, oestrogen, testosterone, and thyroid hormones, and feedback loops involving the hypothalamus and pituitary -- directly mapping to the endocrine system's glands and secretions.

The evolution of lungs covers the mechanics of ventilation, the diaphragm, and vagus nerve control of breathing -- relevant to the lungs and bronchi and the regulation, control, and dynamics of breathing.

Perception and the senses covers visual processing, auditory perception, proprioception, vestibular sense, and multisensory integration -- directly relevant to the eye and vision, ear and audition, and other sensory receptors.

The Eye covers the anatomy of the eye, the mechanism of visual perception, the role of the lens and retina, and visual processing in the brain -- directly relevant to the eye and the process of vision.

The four humours theory (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile) represents the historical understanding of body fluids and their properties, and Galenic medicine's framework for understanding bodily composition.
Pain covers nociception (pain receptors, nerve pathways, brain processing) and the gate control theory -- directly relevant to cutaneous senses including pain.

Anatomy covers the history of anatomical knowledge from Galen through Vesalius, dissection, and understanding of bodily structure -- providing the foundational framework for the supportive-protective system and body cavities.
Enzymes as biological catalysts are essential to the digestive process, with enzyme specificity and regulation directly relevant to the biochemistry of digestion.
Pheromones covers the vomeronasal organ and debates about human pheromones -- directly relevant to the olfactory sense as a sensory receptor.
